item.title: Puget Passive EQ
item.description:
Based on the Pultec EQP-1A program equalizer. The textbook case for why WDF modeling matters. It’s a passive LC equalizer — literally inductors and capacitors in a ladder network — followed by a single 12AX7 tube makeup gain stage. This is the exact circuit topology Wave Digital Filters were invented to model with mathematical precision.

The boost-and-cut trick

The famous “boost and cut at the same frequency” move? It’s not programmed. It’s not a DSP hack. It falls out of the circuit topology naturally — because we modeled the actual passive network, and that’s what passive networks do.

item.title: Puget Opto Compressor
item.description:
Based on the Teletronix LA-2A leveling amplifier. Every major studio has a pair. The most revered optical compressor ever built — two tube gain stages, a passive interstage network, and the T4B electro-optical attenuator that gives it that unmistakable program-dependent character.

The T4B: why it sounds like nothing else

The T4B is a cadmium sulfide photoresistor driven by an electroluminescent panel. Its attack and release behavior is fundamentally nonlinear and program-dependent — loud transients hit harder and recover differently than sustained material.

item.title: Puget FET Limiter
item.description:
Based on the UREI 1176LN limiting amplifier. The fastest compressor in the room. The gain reduction element is a single JFET used as a voltage-variable resistor — which maps naturally to a nonlinear one-port in our WDF tree. The rest is discrete Class A transistor amplifier stages, transformer I/O, and the four ratio settings that change the feedback topology.

All Buttons In

The “All Buttons In” mode — where all four ratio buttons are pressed simultaneously — isn’t a preset. It’s what happens when you wire all four feedback paths in parallel.

item.title: Puget Variable-Mu
item.description:
Based on the Fairchild 670 limiter. The holy grail. Originals sell for $40,000+ when they surface. Twenty tubes, eleven transformers, and the 6386 remote-cutoff triode that makes variable-mu compression possible.

How variable-mu works

The gain reduction happens by biasing push-pull tube stages — as the control voltage changes, the tube’s amplification factor (μ) changes with it. Smooth, musical, and harmonically complex in a way that VCA and FET designs can’t replicate.

item.title: Puget Channel
item.description:
Based on the Neve 1073 channel amplifier. The most recognized preamp in recording history. All transformers and inductors — transformer-coupled Class A discrete preamp, inductor-based 3-band EQ with high-pass filter.

Every component that matters

Every component that gives it that signature sound — the transformers, the hand-wound inductors, the tantalum capacitors — is a WDF element in our model. Nothing is approximated. Nothing is left out.

Real inductors, not filter coefficients

The inductor-based EQ is classic WDF territory. Real inductors have frequency-dependent losses, saturation characteristics, and Q factors that interact with the source impedance. We don’t approximate these — we model the inductor as a physical component with its real-world parameters.

item.title: Puget Studio Collection
item.description:
The five tools that built modern recording. Modeled from the circuit up.

The program equalizer shapes the tone. The FET limiter catches the transients. The opto compressor levels the dynamics. The variable-mu glues the bus. The channel strip does everything in between.

An engineer can mix an entire record with these five tools. Generations of them have.

What’s included

Plugin Circuit Reference Type
Puget Passive EQ EQP-1A style Equalizer
Puget Opto Compressor LA-2A style Compressor
Puget FET Limiter 1176LN style Limiter
Puget Variable-Mu 670 style Compressor/Limiter
Puget Channel 1073 style Preamp/EQ

$699 — Save $296

That’s roughly $140 per plugin vs. $199 individually. Five perpetual licenses, VST3 format, macOS and Windows.

item.title: What Is WDF? The Tech Behind Plugins That Actually Sound Like Real Pedals
item.description:
You’ve probably noticed that some amp sims and pedal plugins sound eerily close to the real thing, while others feel flat and lifeless no matter how much you tweak them. The difference often comes down to how the plugin models the original circuit — and a technology called Wave Digital Filters is quietly behind some of the most realistic-sounding plugins available today.

Here’s what WDF is, why it matters to your tone, and why we use it at Puget Audio.

item.title: Germanium vs Silicon: What Your Diodes Actually Do to Your Signal
item.description:
Every guitarist has an opinion about germanium versus silicon. Germanium is “warm” and “organic.” Silicon is “bright” and “aggressive.” But what are these diodes actually doing to your signal, and why does the material matter so much?

It comes down to a single number, a curve, and the weather.

What a clipping diode does

When your guitar signal is quiet, the diodes in your pedal aren’t doing anything. They’re just sitting there. A diode only starts conducting — letting current flow through it — when the voltage across it exceeds its forward voltage threshold. Below that threshold, it’s basically invisible to your signal.

item.title: Why Your Klon Clone Doesn't Sound Like a Klon
item.description:
The Klon Centaur is the most cloned pedal in history. There are hundreds of versions out there — from $30 Amazon specials to $200 boutique “tributes” — and guitarists have been arguing about whether any of them truly nail the original since the schematic leaked in 2009.

Here’s the thing: most of them get it wrong, and it’s not because they’re using the wrong brand of resistor. It’s because the Klon’s magic lives in its topology — how the circuit is wired — and that’s exactly what most clones simplify away.

item.title: Amp Sag: Why Your Tube Amp Breathes
item.description:
If you’ve ever played through a vintage Fender Deluxe or an old Marshall and felt like the amp was pushing back against you — compressing when you dig in, opening up when you ease off — that’s not your imagination. That’s voltage sag, and it’s one of the biggest reasons tube amps feel alive in a way that solid-state amps and most digital models don’t.

What’s actually happening inside the amp

Your tube amp runs on a high-voltage power supply — typically 300 to 500 volts DC, generated from wall power by a transformer and a rectifier. This voltage (called B+) feeds the plates of every tube in the amp. The tubes need this voltage to amplify your signal.

item.title: The Secret Life of Your Delay Pedal
item.description:
If you’ve ever compared a Boss DM-2 or Electro-Harmonix Memory Man to a clean digital delay, you already know they don’t sound the same. The analog delay is darker, warmer, and slightly unstable — the repeats decay into a murky wash that sits behind your dry signal instead of competing with it.

Most people chalk this up to “analog magic” and leave it at that. But the real story is more interesting. The character of an analog delay comes from a chip you’ve probably never thought about: the compander.

item.title: Products
item.description:
Studio-Grade Audio Tools

We build two kinds of products: software plugins that run in your DAW, and hardware pedals you can bolt to your board. Both share the same DNA — circuit-accurate models built on our Wave Digital Filter engine.

VST3 Plugins

Professional-grade effect emulations for producers, engineers, and guitarists. Each plugin models the actual physics of analog circuits — not impulse responses, not curve fitting, but real component simulation.
